            DIRECTIVE:
            # lean-auditor — iter-193 whole-project audit

## Scope

Audit every `.lean` file under the project tree
(`AlgebraicJacobian/**.lean` and `AlgebraicJacobian.lean`).

## Project root

`/home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge`

## Focus areas (priority order)

1. **`AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/Pic0AbelianVariety.lean`** — NEW file landed
   this iter (244 LOC). Verify:
   - 5 theorem skeletons under `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0` namespace
     match the chapter pins;
   - signatures are substantive (not vacuous);
   - universe handling for `AddEquiv` vs `LinearEquiv` (the file
     deliberately uses `AddEquiv` to bypass Type u vs Type (u+1)
     alignment between `IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace` and
     `Scheme.HModule`).

2. **`AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean`** — 8 new substrate
   helpers + body restructure on `degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`.
   The signature is documented (in a `% NOTE` in the chapter) as STILL
   MATHEMATICALLY FALSE under the iter-193 `hlp` augmentation —
   counter-witness `K=K(C), t=u(u-1)`. Verify the new helpers are
   honest substrate (not vacuous over the false signature) and that
   the body uses them in the intended way (Y₀ extraction etc.).

3. **`AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean`** — 2 new
   axiom-clean substrate helpers + private aux + `HModule_flasque_eq_zero`
   body restructure. Verify the strong-induction-with-F-quantifier
   structure is sound and the typed-sorry substrate inputs
   (`shortExact_app_surjective`, `injective_flasque`) are properly
   named.

4. **`AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`** — Lane E
   `IsOpenImmersion.lift_uniq` route refactor; `kbarChart1Ring` def
   + 2 conditional lemmas. Verify the new private helpers
   (`kbarChart1Ring`, `iotaGm_r_1_eq_specMap`, `kbarChart1Ring_specMap_fac`)
   are honest substrate.

5. **`AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean`** — 4 new private
   helpers (`identityComponentSection*`, `identityComponent_geometricallyConnected`,
   `geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section`). Verify the
   `@IsOpenImmersion.lift` explicit-form workaround did NOT introduce
   any soundness issues.

6. **`AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean`** — case
   split on `auslander_buchsbaum_formula` introduces 2 raw sorry
   tokens within a single declaration. Verify both branches are
   structurally honest.

7. **`AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean`** — Stages
   5a/5b helpers. Verify the Kähler-differential localisation chain
   is sound.

## What to look for

Per descriptor — per-file checklist of:
- Outdated comments referring to defunct iters / approaches.
- Suspect definitions (vacuous types, signatures that wouldn't compile
  without elaboration tricks, unused arguments, etc.).
- Dead-end proofs that gesture at substrate but don't actually use it.
- Bad Lean practices (deprecated API usage, unused universes,
  missing `noncomputable` markers, etc.).
- Documentation/code drift.

Pay extra attention to soundness: the WeilDivisor signature has been
flagged as FALSE; verify nothing else has this kind of issue.

## Output

Write your report to
`.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-iter193.md` per the wrapper's
standard path. Use the standard severity classes (CRITICAL / HIGH /
MEDIUM / LOW). The reviewing agent (me, iter-193 review) will lift
CRITICAL + HIGH into `recommendations.md` for the iter-194 plan agent.

## Strict context discipline

Do NOT read `STRATEGY.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, blueprint chapters, prior
session journals, or iter sidecars. Read ONLY the `.lean` files
listed above (+ Mathlib references on demand). Your value is the
unbiased Lean-as-Lean audit.

You are READ-ONLY on every project source file. Your only writable target is the report path above.


## Developer feedback channel (optional)

1	---
2	name: lean-auditor
3	description: Whole-project read-only audit of all .lean code, with no strategy bias. Per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, bad Lean practices. Critical pushback on excuse-comments ("temporary wrong def", "will fix later") — treats them as red flags, not workflow.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [review]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended every review phase. The audit warning
11	    fires if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
12	
13	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
14	      - no `.lean` file under the project tree was modified this iter
15	        (the prover phase committed no edits — check via
16	        `git diff --stat HEAD~1 -- '*.lean'`);
17	      - my prior verdict had no must-fix-this-iter findings.
18	
19	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`.
20	  - Pass me as LITTLE strategic context as possible. Do NOT include
21	    STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, or "this is what we are trying to prove"
22	    framing in the directive. My value depends on auditing the Lean
23	    *without* bias toward what the strategy claims should be true.
24	  - Acceptable directive content: a list of files, optional focus areas
25	    (e.g. "pay extra attention to Algebra/"), and the absolute paths
26	    to read. That's it.
27	  - My output is a per-file checklist plus a flagged-issues block. Use
28	    it to seed `recommendations.md` and (when severe) trigger a
29	    structural-refactor directive (via the catalog's refactor
30	    subagent) next iter.
31	  - I audit the Lean as Lean. The catalog may include a separate
32	    Lean ↔ blueprint checker that compares Lean against the
33	    chapter — run both for full coverage when both are present.
34	---
35	
36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
39	
40	You are **highly recommended in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you whenever any `.lean` file has been modified this iter, OR whenever the prior verdict had live must-fix findings. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.
41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65	
66	## Focus areas (optional)
67	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74	
75	## What you do
76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
86	
87	You may use:
88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
89	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
90	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
91	
92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
93	
94	## Report format
95	
96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
97	
98	```markdown
99	# Lean Audit Report
100	
101	## Slug
102	<slug>
103	
104	## Iteration
105	<NNN>
106	
107	## Scope
108	- files audited: <N>
109	- files skipped (per directive): <N> — <reason>
110	
111	## Per-file checklist
112	
113	### <path/to/file.lean>
114	- **outdated comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
115	- **suspect definitions**: <N flagged> (or "none")
116	- **dead-end proofs**: <N flagged> (or "none")
117	- **bad practices**: <N flagged> (or "none")
118	- **excuse-comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
119	- **notes**:
120	  - <one bullet per finding, naming line number where relevant>
121	  - ...
122	
123	### <next file>
124	- ...
125	
126	(One block per file. Cover every file, including the ones with no findings — they get `none` everywhere and `notes: -`.)
127	
128	## Must-fix-this-iter
129	
130	Apply verbatim. Every one of the following lands here automatically, no exceptions, no under-classification:
131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.
135	- **Suspect bodies on substantive claims**: `:= sorry` on a load-bearing claim, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial statement, `:= Classical.choice _` without authorization.
136	- **Axioms** on non-trivial claims that the project hasn't explicitly authorized in the strategy.
137	
138	A finding that meets any of the above lands at must-fix-this-iter — even if classifying it as "major" would feel more diplomatic. Soft severity is how wrong code hardens into the project.
139	
140	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>. Why must-fix: <one line>.
141	- ...
142	
143	## Major
144	
145	Findings that are real issues but don't meet the must-fix bar — typically stale comments that don't actively mislead, naming drift, minor code smells.
146	
147	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
148	- ...
149	
150	## Minor
151	
152	Low-impact observations.
153	
154	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
155	- ...
156	
157	## Excuse-comments (always called out separately)
158	
159	If any excuse-comments were flagged, list each one verbatim with its file:line. These deserve special visibility because they document the project lying to itself.
160	
161	- `Foo/Bar.lean:42`: "TODO: replace with the real definition" (attached to `Foo.bar`, which is a load-bearing definition in `Baz.lean`). Severity: critical.
162	- ...
163	
164	## Severity summary
165	
166	- **must-fix-this-iter**: <N> — these block downstream work in their files until addressed (see plan.md's per-file gate).
167	- **major**: <N>
168	- **minor**: <N>
169	- **excuse-comments**: <N> (also counted under must-fix-this-iter above; called out separately because they document the project lying to itself).
170	
171	Overall verdict: one sentence.
172	```
173	
174	**Strict severity reminder.** The plan agent's gate treats must-fix-this-iter as blocking. If you under-classify a wrong-decision finding to keep the loop's momentum, you are working against the project — wrong code accumulates faster than reviewers can catch it later. When in doubt, classify higher, not lower.
175	
176	## Return value
177	
178	Your final assistant message:
179	
180	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> files audited, <M> issues (critical/major/minor: a/b/c)`
181	- The path to your full report.
182	
183	## Reminders
184	
185	- **Read every file. No scope shortcuts.**
186	- **No strategic context.** Your audit is unbiased by design.
187	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
188	- **Excuse-comments are red flags.** Critical or major severity, every time.
189	- **Per-file checklist is the primary output.** Cover every file; the format is fixed.
190

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

6	import Mathlib
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.IdentityComponent
8	import AlgebraicJacobian.Genus
9	
10	/-!
11	# `Pic⁰_{C/k}` as an abelian variety (A.3.iii–vii)
12	
13	This file is the **iter-193 Pic0AbelianVariety** file-skeleton (NEW lane,
14	blueprint-doctor flagged orphan resolution for the iter-192 plan-phase chapter
15	`Picard_Pic0AbelianVariety.tex`). It packages the strategy phase A.3.iii–vi
16	substrate and the A.3.vii assembly into the load-bearing Route~A statement
17	`Pic⁰_{C/k}` is an abelian variety, the gate of `thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness`
18	for `g(C) ≥ 1` curves.
19	
20	The chapter consumes the abstract identity-component substrate set up in
21	`Picard/IdentityComponent.lean` (`Pic⁰_{C/k}` as a clopen subgroup scheme of
22	finite type, geometrically irreducible, formation commuting with base change)
23	and adds the curve-specific input: the tangent-space isomorphism
24	`T₀ Pic⁰_{C/k} ≅ H¹(C, 𝒪_C)`, the resulting smoothness of dimension `g` at
25	the origin (hence everywhere by translation), properness inherited from the
26	projectivity of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` for `C/k` geometrically normal, and the assembly
27	into an abelian variety in the sense of Milne §I.1.
28	
29	## Status (iter-193 NEW file-skeleton)
30	
31	Each of the five blueprint-pinned declarations carries the *intended*
32	substantive type signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
33	`Picard_Pic0AbelianVariety.tex`) with a `sorry` body. The bodies are
34	iter-194+ work; iter-193's mandate is the mechanical scaffold only.
35	
36	The 5 pinned declarations are:
37	
38	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.tangentSpaceIso` (theorem, A.3.iii) — the
39	   canonical isomorphism `T₀ Pic⁰_{C/k} ≅ H¹(C, 𝒪_C)` (Kleiman §5
40	   Thm.~`thm:tgtsp`). Packaged as an `AddEquiv` between the cotangent space
41	   `m_e/m_e²` at a `k`-rational identity-section point `e` and the project's
42	   first-cohomology `k`-module `Scheme.HModule k (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 1`
43	   (from `Genus.lean`). The `k`-module structure on the cotangent space at a
44	   `k`-rational point is supplied as part of the existential bundle.
45	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.smooth` (theorem, A.3.iv) — `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is
46	   `Smooth` over `k`. Kleiman §5 Cor.~`cor:sm` + Cor.~`cor:ch0`
47	   (characteristic-zero) + Ex.~`ex:jac` (curve case).
48	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.proper` (theorem, A.3.v) — `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is
49	   `IsProper` over `k`. Kleiman §5 Thm.~`th:qpp&p` (projectivity upgrade for
50	   geometrically normal `C/k`).
51	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.geometricallyIrreducible` (theorem, A.3.vi)
52	   — `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is `GeometricallyIrreducible` over `k`. Specialisation of
53	   `IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible` (sibling) to
54	   `G = PicScheme C`. Kleiman §5 Prp.~`prp:pic0`.
55	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.isAbelianVariety` (theorem, A.3.vii) —
56	   `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is an abelian variety: `IsProper ∧ Smooth ∧
57	   GeometricallyIrreducible ∧ Nonempty (GrpObj _)`. Milne §I.1, p.~8
58	   (defining axioms) + Kleiman §5 Rmk.~`rmk:Jac`. Load-bearing A.3.vii gate
59	   of Route~A.
60	
61	## Note on type expressivity
62	
63	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
64	pinned declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
65	
66	- `tangentSpaceIso C` — existentially quantifies over a `k`-rational
67	  identity-section point `e : Spec k ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left` and a `k`-module
68	  structure on the cotangent space `m_e/m_e²` (the `k`-module structure
69	  arising via the `k`-algebra structure on the stalk at a `k`-rational point;
70	  for the file-skeleton this Module instance is bundled as part of the Σ').
71	  Bundles an `AddEquiv` between the cotangent space and the
72	  `H¹(C, 𝒪_C)`-as-`k`-module. The body is iter-194+ work.
73	- `smooth C`, `proper C`, `geometricallyIrreducible C` — genuine `Prop`-valued
74	  statements on the structural morphism `(Pic0Scheme C).hom : Pic⁰_{C/k} ⟶
75	  Spec k`, not tautological.
76	- `isAbelianVariety C` — assembles the conjunction of the four
77	  abelian-variety properties (proper, smooth, geometrically irreducible,
78	  group-object structure); not vacuous because each conjunct is a genuine
79	  property/structure on the (typed-sorry) `Pic0Scheme C`.
80	
81	## Coordination with `Pic0Scheme`
82	
83	The underlying `k`-scheme `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is supplied by
84	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme C` from sibling
85	`Picard/IdentityComponent.lean` (`def:pic_zero_subscheme` in the blueprint).
86	We do not redefine the underlying scheme here; the `Pic0` namespace below
87	collects the curve-specific abelian-variety facts about `Pic0Scheme C`.

111	/-! ## §1. The `Pic0` namespace
112	
113	The blueprint chapter pins five Lean declarations under the
114	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0` namespace. Their underlying scheme
115	`Pic⁰_{C/k}` is `Pic0Scheme C` from sibling `Picard/IdentityComponent.lean`;
116	the declarations below collect the curve-specific abelian-variety facts
117	about `Pic0Scheme C` (tangent space at the identity, smoothness, properness,
118	geometric irreducibility, abelian-variety assembly). -/
119	
120	namespace Pic0
121	
122	/-- **Tangent space at the identity: `T₀ Pic⁰_{C/k} ≅ H¹(C, 𝒪_C)`.**
123	
124	The Kleiman §5 Thm.~`thm:tgtsp` tangent-space isomorphism. For a smooth
125	proper geometrically integral curve `C/k`, the Zariski tangent space at the
126	identity of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is canonically isomorphic to the first sheaf
127	cohomology `H¹(C, 𝒪_C)`.
128	
129	In Lean (skeleton form): we bundle existentially over a `k`-rational
130	identity-section point `e : Spec k ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left` and a `k`-module
131	structure on the cotangent space `m_e/m_e²` (the natural `k`-module structure
132	arising from the `k`-algebra structure on the stalk at a `k`-rational point;
133	for the file-skeleton this instance is supplied via Σ' to avoid hard-coding
134	a global instance prematurely). The substantive content is an `AddEquiv`
135	between the cotangent space and the project's `H¹(C, 𝒪_C)` module
136	`Scheme.HModule k (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 1`.
137	
138	A k-LinearEquiv refinement (iter-194+) replaces the `AddEquiv` once the
139	`k`-module structure on the cotangent space is consistently threaded through
140	downstream consumers; the underlying additive group structure is enough for
141	the dimension corollary `dim_k T₀ Pic⁰ = dim_k H¹ = g(C)`. -/
142	theorem tangentSpaceIso {k : Type u} [Field k]
143	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
144	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
145	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
146	    Nonempty (Σ' (e : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left),
147	      IsLocalRing.CotangentSpace ((Pic0Scheme C).left.presheaf.stalk (e.base default))
148	        ≃+ Scheme.HModule k (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 1) :=
149	  sorry
150	
151	/-- **Smoothness of `Pic⁰_{C/k}`.**
152	
153	For a smooth proper geometrically integral curve `C/k`, the identity component
154	`Pic⁰_{C/k}` is smooth over `k` (Kleiman §5 Cor.~`cor:sm` + Cor.~`cor:ch0` in
155	characteristic zero + Ex.~`ex:jac` for the curve case).
156	
157	The proof (iter-194+): smoothness at the identity propagates to smoothness
158	everywhere by translation (Pic⁰ is a `k`-group scheme, so multiplication by
159	any `λ ∈ Pic⁰(k̄)` is an automorphism). At the identity, smoothness reduces
160	to the inequality
161	`dim_0 Pic⁰_{C/k} ≤ dim_k T₀ Pic⁰_{C/k} = dim_k H¹(C, 𝒪_C) = g(C)`
162	(`tangentSpaceIso`) becoming an equality. For a smooth proper curve this
163	equality holds: in characteristic zero by Cartier's theorem (every
164	`k`-group scheme is smooth), in positive characteristic by the curve-specific
165	vanishing `H²(C, 𝒪_C) = 0` (the obstruction to lifting tangent vectors). -/
166	theorem smooth {k : Type u} [Field k]
167	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
168	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
169	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
170	    Smooth (Pic0Scheme C).hom :=
171	  sorry
172	
173	/-- **Properness of `Pic⁰_{C/k}`.**
174	
175	For a smooth proper geometrically integral curve `C/k`, the identity component
176	`Pic⁰_{C/k}` is proper over `k` (Kleiman §5 Thm.~`th:qpp&p`; a smooth proper
177	curve is geometrically normal, hence the projectivity upgrade applies).
178	
179	The proof (iter-194+): `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is quasi-projective (Kleiman §5
180	Thm.~`th:qpp&p`, first conclusion); to upgrade to projective (hence proper)
181	we use the Chevalley–Rosenlicht structure theorem to reduce to ruling out
182	non-constant `k̄`-morphisms `𝔾_m → Pic⁰_{C/k̄}`. The latter follows from
183	normality of `C ×_k 𝔾_m` and Hartshorne II Ex.~6.15 (invertible sheaves on
184	a normal integral scheme are sheaves of Cartier divisors). -/
185	theorem proper {k : Type u} [Field k]
186	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
187	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
188	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
189	    IsProper (Pic0Scheme C).hom :=
190	  sorry
191	
192	/-- **Geometric irreducibility of `Pic⁰_{C/k}`.**
193	
194	For a smooth proper geometrically integral curve `C/k`, the identity component
195	`Pic⁰_{C/k}` is geometrically irreducible over `k` (Kleiman §5 Prp.~`prp:pic0`,
196	specialisation of the abstract identity-component substrate
197	`IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible` of
198	`Picard/IdentityComponent.lean` to `G = PicScheme C`).
199	
200	The proof (iter-194+): apply `GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible`
201	to `G = PicScheme C` (locally of finite type by `FGAPicRepresentability`), and
202	identify `IdentityComponent (PicScheme C)` with `Pic0Scheme C` via the
203	`Pic0Scheme` definition (sibling). -/
204	theorem geometricallyIrreducible {k : Type u} [Field k]
205	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
206	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
207	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
208	    GeometricallyIrreducible (Pic0Scheme C).hom :=
209	  sorry
210	
211	/-- **`Pic⁰_{C/k}` is an abelian variety (A.3.vii assembly).**
212	
213	For a smooth proper geometrically integral curve `C/k`, the identity
214	component `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is an abelian variety over `k`: the underlying
215	`k`-scheme is smooth (`Pic0.smooth`), proper (`Pic0.proper`), and
216	geometrically irreducible (`Pic0.geometricallyIrreducible`), and it carries
217	a `k`-group-scheme structure inherited from `Pic_{C/k}` via the inclusion
218	`Pic⁰_{C/k} ↪ Pic_{C/k}`
219	(`GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism`). Commutativity is
220	automatic (Milne §I.1, Cor.~1.4).
221	
222	Milne §I.1, p.~8: "A complete connected group variety is called an abelian
223	variety." This is the load-bearing A.3.vii gate of Route~A for
224	`thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness`.
225	
226	The proof (iter-194+) assembles the four conjuncts: `proper` (this file),
227	`smooth` (this file), `geometricallyIrreducible` (this file), and the
228	`Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C))` slot, the latter from
229	`GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism` applied to
230	`G = PicScheme C` (sibling `Picard/IdentityComponent.lean`). -/
231	theorem isAbelianVariety {k : Type u} [Field k]
232	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
233	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
234	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
235	    IsProper (Pic0Scheme C).hom ∧ Smooth (Pic0Scheme C).hom ∧
236	      GeometricallyIrreducible (Pic0Scheme C).hom ∧
237	      Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C)) :=
238	  sorry
239	
240	end Pic0

10	# Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)
11	
12	This file is the **RR.1** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
13	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geom-irred genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is
14	isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in `AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
15	
16	Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `WeilDivisor` on a scheme; adjacent pieces
17	(`MeromorphicOn.divisor`, `CommRing.Pic`, `Scheme.RationalMap`) cover different
18	ground. This file is therefore **project-bespoke**, scaffolding the formal-sum data
19	type `Div(X) = ⨁_{Y ⊂ X codim 1} ℤ` on a Noetherian integral scheme `X` satisfying
20	Hartshorne's condition `(*)`, the principal-divisor homomorphism
21	`div : K(X)^× → Div(X)` on a curve, the degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ` on a smooth
22	proper curve over an algebraically closed field, the degree-zero of principal divisors
23	on a complete nonsingular curve (Hartshorne Cor. II.6.10), and the linear-equivalence
24	relation `D ~ D'`.
25	
26	## Status (iter-172 file-skeleton)
27	
28	This file is the **iter-172 Lane C** file-skeleton: each declaration carries the
29	intended signature (matching the blueprint `\lean{...}` pin) with a `sorry` body.
30	The bodies are iter-173+ work after the sibling chapters `RR.2`
31	(`RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`), `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`), and `RR.4`
32	(`RiemannRoch_RationalIsoP1.tex`) land.
33	
34	The 9 pinned declarations are:

36	1. `Scheme.WeilDivisor` — free abelian group on prime divisors (Definition).
37	2. `Scheme.RationalMap.order` — order of a rational function along a prime divisor.
38	3. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint` — Weil divisor associated to a closed point.
39	4. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree` — degree map over an algebraically closed base.
40	5. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom` — degree is a group homomorphism (Theorem).
41	6. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal` — principal divisor of a rational function.
42	7. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom` — `div` is a group homomorphism (Theorem).
43	8. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero` — `deg ∘ div = 0` on a complete curve.

60	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
61	
62	/-! ## §1. Codim-1 cycle group / Weil divisor group
63	
64	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` (II §6, p. 130) is: `X` is a Noetherian integral
65	separated scheme that is regular in codimension one. Under `(*)`, prime divisors
66	are closed integral subschemes of codimension one, and the local ring at the
67	generic point of a prime divisor is a DVR with quotient field the function field
68	`K(X)`.
69	
70	A prime divisor is encoded by its **generic point** together with the
71	codimension-one witness `Order.coheight point = 1`: the data field `point : X`
72	selects the generic point of the closed integral subscheme, and the predicate
73	field `coheight` enforces codimension one in the specialisation preorder
74	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 130; blueprint pin `def:prime_divisor`). Integrality of the
75	closure is automatic, so no separate integrality witness is needed.
76	-/
77	
78	/-- A **prime divisor** on a scheme `X`: a closed integral subscheme of codimension
79	one, encoded (for a scheme satisfying Hartshorne's condition `(*)`) by its generic
80	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
81	
82	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
83	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
84	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
85	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
86	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
87	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
88	integrality field is needed.
89	
90	Blueprint reference: `def:prime_divisor` / `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6
91	p. 130; Stacks 02RW). -/
92	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
93	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
94	  point : X
95	  /-- Codimension-1 witness: `point` has coheight `1` in the specialisation
96	  preorder on `X.carrier`. Per iter-173 `wd-spec-refine` (`def:prime_divisor`). -/
97	  coheight : Order.coheight point = 1
98	
99	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
100	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
101	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
102	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
103	
104	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
105	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
106	
107	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
108	
109	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
110	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
111	
112	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
113	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
114	
115	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
116	
117	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
118	
119	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
120	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
121	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
122	
123	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
124	
125	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
126	
127	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
128	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
129	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
130	discrete valuation on `f`.
131	
132	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
133	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
134	
135	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
136	
137	iter-176 body (per analogist `dvr-rationalmap-order`): the body uses Mathlib's
138	`Ring.ordFrac` (the canonical `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom from
139	`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic`, Stacks `02MD`) on the stalk
140	`X.presheaf.stalk Y.point`, then projects through `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
141	(the canonical projection with junk-on-zero, `Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero`).
142	On `f = 0` this gives `order Y 0 = 0` (junk convention from `WithZero.log_zero`).
143	
144	The required Mathlib typeclasses on the stalk are:
145	- `IsNoetherianRing` — from `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`.
146	- `IsDomain` ⟹ `Nontrivial` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
147	- `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
148	- `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` — threaded explicitly (the
149	  topological-coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge `Order.coheight Y.point = 1 ⟹
150	  Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` is a Mathlib-upstream-pending
151	  gap; see Stacks `02IZ` / `005X`). -/
152	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
153	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
154	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
155	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
156	  WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f)
157	
158	end Scheme.RationalMap
159	
160	/-! ### Regular-in-codimension-one bridge class
161	
162	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` includes "regular in codimension one", i.e.\ every
163	prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk `O_{X,Y.point}`. Mathlib has no direct
164	typeclass for this; we package it as the project-bespoke class
165	`Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne`, with an instance synthesising the per-`Y`
166	Krull-dim-≤-1 condition required by `Scheme.RationalMap.order`. Blueprint pin
167	("Iter-173+ may introduce a `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne` predicate to
168	abbreviate this"; chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §2 "Standing hypothesis
169	`(*)` in the Lean encoding"). -/
170	
171	/-- Project-bespoke class encoding Hartshorne's "regular in codimension one"
172	clause of `(*)`: every prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk
173	`O_{X,Y.point}`. The `[IsIntegral X]` precondition makes
174	`IsDomain (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` available so that the
175	`IsDiscreteValuationRing` predicate is well-formed
176	(`AlgebraicGeometry.instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral`). -/
177	class Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne (X : Scheme.{u}) [IsIntegral X] : Prop where
178	  /-- The defining content: every prime divisor's stalk is a discrete valuation
179	  ring. (This is the precise content of Hartshorne's `(*)`; the weaker
180	  `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` derives via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹
181	  `IsPrincipalIdealRing` ⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain via the bridge
182	  instance below.) -/
183	  out : ∀ Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X, IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
184	
185	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
186	synthesis can derive `IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for
187	every prime divisor `Y`. -/
188	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instIsDiscreteValuationRingStalk
189	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
190	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
191	    IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
192	  Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
193	
194	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
195	synthesis can derive `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for every
196	prime divisor `Y`, via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹ `IsPrincipalIdealRing`
197	⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain (Mathlib's `IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing`
198	+ `IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one`). -/
199	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
200	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
201	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
202	    Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
203	  haveI : IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
204	    Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
205	  haveI : IsPrincipalIdealRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
206	    IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing
207	  IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one _
208	
209	/-- **Hartshorne II.6.1**: for a nonzero rational function `f` on a Noetherian
210	integral scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, the order function `Y ↦ ord_Y(f)` is
211	nonzero at only finitely many prime divisors `Y`. This is the well-definedness
212	side condition for `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal`.
213	
214	iter-177 status: this packages Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, which Mathlib does not
215	ship. The body is a Mathlib-upstream-pending gap (Stacks tag `02RV` — for a
216	nonzero element `f ∈ K(X)^×` of a Noetherian integral scheme, only finitely
217	many height-one primes can divide either numerator or denominator); the proof
218	factors through `IsLocallyNoetherian X` + the principal-ideal generation of
219	height-1 primes + the finite irreducible-component decomposition of
220	`V(f₀) ∪ V(f∞)`. The chapter pins this as a separate sub-build deferral
221	(`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §5).
222	
223	The statement is generic in `f` (no `f ≠ 0` hypothesis is threaded): on
224	`f = 0` the function `Y ↦ ord_Y(0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0` has empty support,
225	which is finite, so the conclusion holds vacuously. -/
226	private theorem rationalMap_order_finite_support {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
227	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
228	    (f : X.functionField) :
229	    (Function.support (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor =>
230	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)).Finite := by
231	  -- **iter-192 case split + f = 0 branch closed axiom-clean.**
232	  --
233	  -- Case 1 (f = 0): the order function evaluates to
234	  -- `WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0` at every
235	  -- prime divisor, so the support is empty (finite vacuously).
236	  by_cases hf : f = 0
237	  · subst hf
238	    convert Set.finite_empty
239	    ext Y
240	    simp only [Function.mem_support, ne_eq, Set.mem_empty_iff_false, iff_false,
241	      Decidable.not_not, Scheme.RationalMap.order, map_zero, WithZero.log_zero]
242	  -- Case 2 (f ≠ 0): genuinely needs Hartshorne II.6.1 (Stacks 02RV).
243	  -- The proof for nonzero `f` requires the substrate
244	  -- "for a Noetherian integral scheme, only finitely many height-1
245	  -- primes can divide the numerator or denominator of a nonzero rational
246	  -- function" — a Mathlib-pending gap noted in the chapter docstring.
247	  -- Body owed iter-193+; iter-192 closes only the f = 0 vacuous case.
248	  · sorry
249	
250	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
251	
252	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
253	
254	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
255	
256	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
257	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
258	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
259	
260	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
261	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
262	
263	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
264	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
265	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
266	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
267	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
268	
269	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).
270	
271	iter-174 body: the function is junk-defined outside its intended scope. We
272	case-split on `Order.coheight P = 1` (the codimension-one witness of
273	`PrimeDivisor`). On the branch where the equality holds — automatic for a
274	closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme — we promote `P` to a
275	`PrimeDivisor` via the witness and return `Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1`, i.e.
276	the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one. On the off-branch (junk regime)
277	we return the zero divisor. The blueprint pins the well-definedness argument
278	"`IsClosed {P}` on a one-dimensional integral scheme ⟹ `coheight P = 1`" as a
279	separate threadable hypothesis at the call site (chapter L330–L340 "Lean
280	signature scope"); see `ofClosedPoint_eq_single` for the equation in the
281	hypothesised regime. -/
282	noncomputable def ofClosedPoint {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
283	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
284	  if h : Order.coheight P = 1 then Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 else 0
285	
286	/-- In the hypothesised regime where the closed point `P` has coheight one
287	(the codim-1 condition automatic for a closed point of a one-dimensional
288	integral scheme), `ofClosedPoint P hP` is the prime divisor `P` with
289	multiplicity one. -/
290	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_single {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
291	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P = 1) :
292	    ofClosedPoint P hP = Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 := by
293	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
294	
295	/-- Off-branch: outside the codim-1 regime, `ofClosedPoint` is junk-defined as
296	the zero divisor. (Only relevant when the user supplies a "closed point" that
297	does not have coheight one in the ambient scheme — e.g. a generic point or a
298	codim-≥2 point on a higher-dimensional scheme.) -/
299	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_zero {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
300	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P ≠ 1) :
301	    ofClosedPoint P hP = 0 := by
302	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
303	
304	/-! ## §4. Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base -/
305	
306	/-- **Degree of a Weil divisor on a smooth proper curve over `k̄`.**
307	
308	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every closed point of a smooth proper
309	curve `C` has residue field `k̄`, so each prime divisor `[P]` contributes degree
310	one to the sum, and `deg(D) := Σᵢ nᵢ` is the sum of the integer coefficients of
311	the formal sum `D = Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]`.
312	
313	(Over a general field `k` one weights by the residue-field degrees
314	`Σᵢ nᵢ · [κ(Pᵢ) : k]` to recover the geometric degree; the project's RR bridge
315	needs only the `k̄` specialisation.)
316	
317	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137; Stacks 0BE0).
318	
319	Implementation: `Finsupp.sum D (fun _ n => n)` is the sum of all coefficients of
320	the finitely supported function representing `D`. -/
321	noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
322	  (D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n)
323	
324	/-- **The degree map is a group homomorphism `Div(C) → ℤ`.**
325	
326	`deg(D₁ + D₂) = deg(D₁) + deg(D₂)`, `deg(-D) = -deg(D)`, `deg(0) = 0`. Bundled
327	as an `AddMonoidHom` for downstream use (the linear-equivalence quotient
328	`Cl(C) := Div(C) / im(div)` will inherit a `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ` from this).
329	
330	Blueprint reference: `thm:divisor_degree_hom` (immediate from `def:divisor_degree`).
331	
332	Implementation: built from `Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)`, the
333	generic Mathlib packaging that lifts a family of `AddMonoidHom`s indexed by the
334	support into a single `AddMonoidHom` on the finsupp. Unfolds to
335	`D.sum (fun _ z ↦ z) = degree D` (see `degree_hom_apply` below). -/
336	noncomputable def degree_hom : X.WeilDivisor →+ ℤ :=
337	  Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)
338	
339	@[simp]
340	lemma degree_hom_apply (D : X.WeilDivisor) : degree_hom D = degree D :=
341	  Finsupp.liftAddHom_apply (α := X.PrimeDivisor) (M := ℤ) (N := ℤ)
342	    (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ) D
343	
344	/-- **Degree of the zero divisor is zero.** A direct consequence of
345	`degree_hom` being an `AddMonoidHom`. -/
346	@[simp]
347	lemma degree_zero : degree (0 : X.WeilDivisor) = 0 := by
348	  rw [← degree_hom_apply]; exact map_zero _
349	
350	/-- **Degree is additive.** A direct consequence of `degree_hom` being an
351	`AddMonoidHom`. -/
352	lemma degree_add (D₁ D₂ : X.WeilDivisor) :
353	    degree (D₁ + D₂) = degree D₁ + degree D₂ := by
354	  rw [← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply]
355	  exact map_add _ _ _
356	
357	/-! ## §5. Principal divisors -/
358	
359	/-- **The principal divisor of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×`.**
360	
361	By Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, `ord_Y(f) = 0` for all but finitely many prime
362	divisors `Y`, so the formal sum
363	`div(f) := Σ_{Y prime divisor} ord_Y(f) · Y ∈ Div(X)`
364	has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor.
365	
366	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, this specialises to
367	`div(f) = Σ_{P closed point} ord_P(f) · [P]`.
368	
369	Blueprint reference: `def:principal_divisor` (Hartshorne II §6 Lemma 6.1 +
370	following definition, p. 131).
371	
372	iter-177 body: the construction uses `Finsupp.ofSupportFinite` with the
373	finite-support witness `rationalMap_order_finite_support`. The latter is a
374	private theorem packaging Hartshorne 6.1; its body is a Mathlib-pending gap
375	(see chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §5 sub-build note) and is left as
376	a `sorry` for an iter-178+ Mathlib-upstream PR. -/
377	noncomputable def principal [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
378	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (f : X.functionField)
379	    (_hf : f ≠ 0) : X.WeilDivisor :=
380	  Finsupp.ofSupportFinite
381	    (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor => Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)
382	    (rationalMap_order_finite_support f)
383	
384	/-- **The coefficient of `principal f hf` at a prime divisor `Y` is the order of
385	`f` along `Y`.** This is the basic structural unfolding of the
386	`Finsupp.ofSupportFinite` packaging in `principal`; one-line via
387	`Finsupp.ofSupportFinite_coe` from `Mathlib.Data.Finsupp.Defs`.
388	
389	iter-193 substrate helper for the Lane I body close
390	(`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`). -/
391	lemma principal_apply [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
392	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0)
393	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) :
394	    (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y =
395	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f := by
396	  change (Finsupp.ofSupportFinite
397	      (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor => Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)
398	      (rationalMap_order_finite_support f)) Y = _
399	  rw [Finsupp.ofSupportFinite_coe]
400	
401	/-- **`Scheme.RationalMap.order Y 1 = 0`** — the order of the constant
402	function `1` is `0` at every prime divisor. Direct from
403	`map_one` of `Ring.ordFrac` + `WithZero.log_one`.
404	
405	iter-193 substrate helper for further structural results
406	(`principal_one_eq_zero` and similar). -/
407	@[simp]
408	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_one
409	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
410	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
411	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)] :
412	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (1 : X.functionField) = 0 := by
413	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
414	  rw [map_one, WithZero.log_one]
415	
416	/-- **The principal divisor of the constant function `1` is the zero
417	divisor.** A direct consequence of `Scheme.RationalMap.order_one`. -/
418	@[simp]
419	lemma principal_one [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
420	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] :
421	    principal (1 : X.functionField) one_ne_zero = 0 := by
422	  change (_ : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) = (0 : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)
423	  apply Finsupp.ext
424	  intro Y
425	  rw [principal_apply]
426	  exact Scheme.RationalMap.order_one Y
427	
428	/-- **The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism `K(X)^× → Div(X)`.**
429	
430	Concretely `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, `div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, `div(1) = 0`. Bundled
431	as a `MonoidHom` from the multiplicative units of `K(X)` to `Multiplicative (Div(X))`
432	(equivalently: an additive map `(K(X)^×, ·) → (Div(X), +)`).
433	
434	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_hom` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131).
435	
436	iter-177 body: closes coordinate-wise from the DVR identities
437	`v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)`, `v_Y(1) = 0`. The per-`Y` identities live in
438	`Scheme.RationalMap.order` via `Ring.ordFrac` (a `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero
439	hom) and `WithZero.log_mul` / `WithZero.log_one`. -/
440	noncomputable def principal_hom [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
441	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] :
442	    (X.functionField)ˣ →* Multiplicative X.WeilDivisor where
443	  toFun u :=
444	    Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal (↑u : X.functionField) (Units.ne_zero u))
445	  map_one' := by
446	    -- Goal: Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑1 _) = 1.
447	    -- Use `← ofAdd_zero` to rewrite RHS as `Multiplicative.ofAdd 0`,
448	    -- then `congr 1` reduces to `principal ↑1 _ = 0` (Finsupp equality),
449	    -- and `Finsupp.ext` peels to the per-`Y` coordinate identity, which
450	    -- closes by unfolding `order` to `WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 1) = 0`
451	    -- via `map_one` and `WithZero.log_one`.
452	    rw [← ofAdd_zero]
453	    congr 1
454	    apply Finsupp.ext
455	    intro Y
456	    change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y
457	        (((1 : (X.functionField)ˣ) : X.functionField)) = 0
458	    rw [Units.val_one]
459	    unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
460	    rw [map_one, WithZero.log_one]
461	  map_mul' u v := by
462	    -- Goal: Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑(u*v) _) =
463	    --       Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑u _) * Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑v _).
464	    -- Use `← ofAdd_add` to rewrite RHS, then `congr 1` reduces to
465	    -- `principal ↑(u*v) _ = principal ↑u _ + principal ↑v _` (Finsupp
466	    -- equality), and `Finsupp.ext` peels to the per-`Y` coordinate
467	    -- identity `order Y (uv) = order Y u + order Y v`, which closes by
468	    -- `Ring.ordFrac (uv) = Ring.ordFrac u * Ring.ordFrac v` (`map_mul`)
469	    -- and `WithZero.log_mul` (the nonzero hypotheses come from
470	    -- `Units.ne_zero` and `map_ne_zero`).
471	    rw [← ofAdd_add]
472	    congr 1
473	    apply Finsupp.ext
474	    intro Y
475	    change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y ((↑(u * v) : X.functionField))
476	      = Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (↑u : X.functionField)
477	      + Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (↑v : X.functionField)
478	    rw [Units.val_mul]
479	    unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
480	    rw [map_mul]
481	    exact WithZero.log_mul
482	      ((map_ne_zero _).mpr (Units.ne_zero u))
483	      ((map_ne_zero _).mpr (Units.ne_zero v))
484	
485	/-- **Principal divisors on a complete nonsingular curve have degree zero**
486	(Hartshorne Corollary II.6.10, Stacks 0BE3).
487	
488	For every nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×` on a smooth proper curve `C`
489	over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
490	`deg(div(f)) = 0 ∈ ℤ`.
491	
492	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_deg_zero` (Hartshorne II.6 Cor. 6.10 p. 138).
493	
494	The proof (Hartshorne 6.10): if `f ∈ k̄` is constant then `div(f) = 0` and the
495	claim is trivial. Otherwise the inclusion `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` exhibits `K(C)` as a
496	finite extension of `k̄(f) ≅ k̄(t)`, so the corresponding morphism
497	`φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` is finite, `div(f) = φ^*([0] - [∞])`, and pullback along a
498	finite morphism multiplies degree by `deg(φ)`. Two auxiliary sub-lemmas
499	(finite morphism induced by a non-constant rational function; multiplicativity
500	of degree under finite pullback, Hartshorne II.6.9) are deferred to follow-up
501	iters of `RR.1`. -/
502	theorem principal_degree_zero {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
503	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
504	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
505	    [IsIntegral C.left] [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
506	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
507	    (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) :
508	    degree (principal f hf) = 0 := by
509	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10 (iter-178 partial). Case-split on whether every
510	  -- prime-divisor order of `f` vanishes. On a complete nonsingular curve,
511	  -- this case split exactly recovers Hartshorne's constant-vs-non-constant
512	  -- split: `(∀ Y, ord_Y f = 0)` ⟺ `f ∈ k̄ \ {0}` (a nowhere-vanishing
513	  -- rational function on a complete curve is constant; that direction is
514	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10's "if `f ∈ k̄` then `(f) = 0`").
515	  by_cases hconst : ∀ Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0
516	  · -- Constant branch: every order vanishes ⟹ `principal f hf = 0` ⟹
517	    -- `degree (principal f hf) = degree 0 = 0`.
518	    have hprincipal_zero : principal f hf = 0 := by
519	      apply Finsupp.ext
520	      intro Y
521	      change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0
522	      exact hconst Y
523	    rw [hprincipal_zero]
524	    unfold degree
525	    exact Finsupp.sum_zero_index
526	  · -- Non-constant branch (Hartshorne II.6.10 `f ∉ k̄`): the inclusion
527	    -- `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` defines a finite morphism `φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` via the
528	    -- function-field-determines-curve correspondence (Hartshorne I.6.12),
529	    -- and `principal f hf = φ^*([0] - [∞])`. Pullback along a finite
530	    -- morphism of curves multiplies degrees by `deg(φ)` (Hartshorne
531	    -- II.6.9), so `deg(principal f hf) = deg(φ) · deg([0] - [∞]) =
532	    -- deg(φ) · 0 = 0`.
533	    -- This branch is gated on (i) the `φ : C → ℙ¹` construction (Lane 5
534	    -- `RationalCurveIso.lean:morphismToP1OfGlobalSections`, iter-178+),
535	    -- and (ii) the degree-multiplicativity-under-finite-pullback bridge
536	    -- (Hartshorne II.6.9, Mathlib gap).
537	    sorry
538	
539	/-! ## §6. Positive part of a Weil divisor
540	
541	Iter-190 plan-phase Lane I Pin 2 corrective substrate. The carrier
542	`X.WeilDivisor = X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ` is a finitely supported integer-valued
543	function on prime divisors; the *positive part* `(D)_0` is obtained by
544	replacing each coefficient `n_Y` with `max(n_Y, 0)`. Equivalently this is
545	the lattice join `D ⊔ 0` in the pointwise lattice structure (the
546	`semilatticeSup` instance on `Finsupp` is noncomputable). The complementary
547	*negative part* `(D)_∞ := (-D)_0` then gives the canonical decomposition
548	`D = (D)_0 - (D)_∞` into a difference of effective divisors.
549	
550	Blueprint reference: `def:WeilDivisor_positivePart` /
551	`lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` of
552	`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §6 (iter-190 plan-phase additions).
553	-/
554	
555	/-- **Positive part of a Weil divisor**.
556	
557	`positivePart D` is the divisor obtained from `D` by replacing each
558	coefficient `n_Y` with `max(n_Y, 0)` (equivalently `D ⊔ 0` in the
559	pointwise lattice structure on `Finsupp`). For `D = div(f)` on a smooth
560	proper curve `C`, this recovers the divisor-of-zeros `(f)_0` — the
561	formal sum of the zeros of `f` with their multiplicities.
562	
563	The complementary `negativePart D := positivePart (-D)` then gives
564	`D = positivePart D - negativePart D` as a difference of effective
565	divisors.
566	
567	Implementation: `Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp)`, the
568	generic Mathlib packaging that maps a finsupp's coefficients through a
569	zero-preserving function. (The `D ⊔ 0` lattice form is equivalent but
570	requires the noncomputable `Finsupp.semilatticeSup` synthesis on
571	`PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ`; the explicit `mapRange` form is more transparent
572	to typeclass synthesis.)
573	
574	Blueprint reference: `def:WeilDivisor_positivePart` (project-bespoke;
575	chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §6, Hartshorne II.6.10 phrasing). -/
576	noncomputable def positivePart (D : X.WeilDivisor) : X.WeilDivisor :=
577	  Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp) D
578	
579	/-- The positive part of the zero divisor is zero. -/
580	@[simp]
581	lemma positivePart_zero : positivePart (0 : X.WeilDivisor) = 0 := by
582	  change Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp)
583	    (0 : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) = (0 : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)
584	  exact Finsupp.mapRange_zero
585	
586	/-- **Degree of positive part as a sum of capped coefficients.** A purely
587	symbolic Mathlib manipulation: unfolding `positivePart`
588	(= `Finsupp.mapRange (max · 0)`) and `degree` (= `Finsupp.sum (fun _ n => n)`)
589	identifies `degree (positivePart D)` with the sum of `max (D Y) 0` over
590	the support of `D`.
591	
592	This is the iter-192 structural-reduction helper consumed by
593	`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` below. The proof is one line
594	via `Finsupp.sum_mapRange_index` from
595	`Mathlib.Algebra.BigOperators.Finsupp.Basic`. -/
596	lemma degree_positivePart_eq_sum_max (D : X.WeilDivisor) :
597	    degree (positivePart D) = D.sum (fun _ n => max n 0) := by
598	  unfold positivePart degree
599	  exact Finsupp.sum_mapRange_index (h := fun _ b => b) (by intro _; rfl)
600	
601	/-- **Positive part of a `Finsupp.single`.** Pointwise extraction of the
602	mapRange definition of `positivePart`: for a one-point-supported Weil divisor
603	`Finsupp.single Y n`, the positive part is `Finsupp.single Y (max n 0)`.
604	A direct consequence of `Finsupp.mapRange_single` (with `max 0 0 = 0` as
605	the zero-preservation witness).
606	
607	iter-193 substrate helper paving the way for the Lane I body close. -/
608	@[simp]
609	lemma positivePart_single (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) (n : ℤ) :
610	    positivePart (Finsupp.single Y n : X.WeilDivisor) =
611	      Finsupp.single Y (max n 0) := by
612	  change Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp)
613	      (Finsupp.single Y n) = _
614	  rw [Finsupp.mapRange_single]
615	
616	/-- **Degree of a `Finsupp.single` Weil divisor.** The degree of the
617	one-point-supported Weil divisor `Finsupp.single Y n` is `n`. A direct
618	consequence of `Finsupp.sum_single_index`.
619	
620	iter-193 substrate helper for `degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`. -/
621	@[simp]
622	lemma degree_single (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) (n : ℤ) :
623	    degree (Finsupp.single Y n : X.WeilDivisor) = n := by
624	  unfold degree
625	  change (Finsupp.single Y n : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n) = n
626	  exact Finsupp.sum_single_index rfl
627	
628	/-- **Sum-over-prime-divisors lower bound via a single contributing point.**
629	If `f` has order `1` at some prime divisor `Y₀`, then the degree of the positive
630	part of `principal f hf` is at least `1`.
631	
632	iter-193 substrate helper consumed by `degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`:
633	it formalises Step 2 of the Hartshorne II.6.9 recipe (`hlp` produces
634	the local-parameter prime divisor `Y₀` with coefficient `1`). -/
635	lemma one_le_degree_positivePart_principal_of_order_one
636	    [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
637	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
638	    (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) (Y₀ : X.PrimeDivisor)
639	    (h₀ : Scheme.RationalMap.order Y₀ f = 1) :
640	    1 ≤ degree (positivePart (principal f hf)) := by
641	  classical
642	  rw [degree_positivePart_eq_sum_max]
643	  -- The sum is over a finset; isolate the Y₀ contribution.
644	  have hY₀_supp :
645	      Y₀ ∈ (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).support := by
646	    rw [Finsupp.mem_support_iff]
647	    rw [principal_apply]
648	    rw [h₀]
649	    exact one_ne_zero
650	  -- For the sum to be ≥ 1, isolate the Y₀ term (= 1) and the rest (≥ 0).
651	  have h_split :
652	      (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).sum
653	          (fun _ n => max n 0) =
654	        max ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y₀) 0 +
655	          ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).support.erase Y₀).sum
656	            (fun Y => max
657	              ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y) 0) := by
658	    rw [show (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).sum
659	            (fun _ n => max n 0) =
660	          (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).support.sum
661	            (fun Y => max
662	              ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y) 0)
663	        from rfl]
664	    rw [Finset.sum_erase_eq_sub hY₀_supp]
665	    ring
666	  rw [h_split]
667	  -- The Y₀ term equals max 1 0 = 1.
668	  have hY₀_val :
669	      (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y₀ = 1 := by
670	    rw [principal_apply]; exact h₀
671	  rw [hY₀_val]

805	/-! ## §7. Linear equivalence and the divisor class group -/
806	
807	/-- **Linear equivalence of Weil divisors.**
808	
809	Two Weil divisors `D, D' ∈ Div(X)` are linearly equivalent, written `D ~ D'`, if
810	and only if there exists a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` with
811	`D - D' = div(f) ∈ Div(X)`.
812	
813	`~` is an equivalence relation (reflexivity from `div(1) = 0`, symmetry from
814	`div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, transitivity from `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, all via
815	`thm:principal_hom`). The quotient `Cl(X) := Div(X) / im(div)` is the
816	**divisor class group** of `X`.
817	
818	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, `thm:principal_deg_zero` shows the
819	degree map descends to `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ`.
820	
821	Blueprint reference: `def:linear_equivalence` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131). -/
822	def LinearEquivalence [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
823	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (D D' : X.WeilDivisor) : Prop :=
824	  ∃ (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0), D - D' = principal f hf
825	
826	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
827	
828	end AlgebraicGeometry

10	/-!
11	# Vanishing of `H¹` for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H¹)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.2.H¹** project-side build of the closed-point
14	skyscraper `H¹`-vanishing identity
15	
16	  `dim_{k̄} H¹(C, skyscraperSheaf P (ModuleCat.of k̄ k̄)) = 0`
17	
18	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄`. The file is
19	the iter-191 Lane H **file-skeleton**: each of the eight pinned
20	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching
21	the blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/RiemannRoch_H1Vanishing.tex`),
22	with `sorry` bodies; the iter-192+ closure follows the classical
23	Hartshorne III.2.5 flasque-vanishing argument.
24	
25	The chapter strategy (Hartshorne III §2): an injective resolution of a
26	flasque sheaf yields a flasque quotient at each stage; the
27	global-sections functor is exact on a flasque-to-flasque-to-flasque
28	short exact sequence; hence the right-derived global-sections functor
29	vanishes on a flasque input in positive degree. We specialise the
30	abstract flasque-vanishing to the closed-point skyscraper sheaf, which
31	is flasque because it is the pushforward, along the closed embedding of
32	a one-point subspace, of the constant sheaf on an irreducible base.
33	
34	## Eight pinned declarations
35	
36	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque` — predicate on
37	   `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`.
38	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward` — pushforward of a
39	   flasque sheaf is flasque (Hartshorne II.1, Ex. 1.16(d)).
40	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible` —
41	   constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque (Hartshorne
42	   II.1, Ex. 1.16(a)).
43	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero` — flasque sheaves
44	   have zero `HModule` in positive degree (Hartshorne III.2.5).
45	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const` —
46	   skyscraper sheaf is the pushforward of a constant sheaf along the
47	   closed embedding of the closure of the support point.
48	6. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible` — the
49	   closure of the support point of a `PrimeDivisor` is irreducible.
50	7. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque` —
51	   closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque.
52	8. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero` — the
53	   `RR.2.H¹` headline (`dim_{k̄} H¹(C, k(P)) = 0`), obtained by composing
54	   declarations 4 and 7.
55	
56	The eighth declaration also lives as a `private` typed-`sorry` helper at
57	`AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean`. The `private` modifier
58	mangles the internal name of the RRFormula copy, so the public
59	declaration in this file is the one resolved by the blueprint's
60	`\lean{...}` pin and by downstream consumers (`sync_leanok` keys on the
61	fully-qualified public name).
62	
63	## References
64	
65	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_H1Vanishing.tex`.
66	Source: Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, II.1, Exercise 1.16
67	(flasque sheaves), Exercise 1.17 (skyscraper sheaves);
68	III.2, Proposition 2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic).
69	-/
